category_title: Students with behavioral difficulties and fragmented self-concept following personal trauma
minimal_context: Archie is completing a reflective journal entry about his personal history during a one-to-one session.
dialogue:
TUTOR: Please open your journal so we can complete your reflection on how things have been going recently and your personal history.

STUDENT: I've been feeling really really happy. My confidence has definitely gone up.

TUTOR: Okay, write that down. What else comes to mind?

STUDENT: Well, before the accident I was doing my hobbies... but then it happened. I'm just slow now. I used to just feel awful.

TUTOR: Keep going with your reflection.

STUDENT: It's a struggle. I don't know who I am anymore without my hobbies. It's just a weighty depression and I'm worried. I keep, falling into depression.

options.A: Encourage the student to complete homework after every session, which involves practicing new techniques and completing a journal.
options.B: Ensure teachers do not routinely direct or allow tutors to withdraw the student from the classroom for these sessions.
options.C: Rotate one-to-one tutor assignments annually, or adopt a shared-tutor model with daily or weekly rotations for these sessions.
options.D: Avoid forcing the student to write about traumatic experiences in the journal; rather, provide them with a choice of topics.
